{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56112","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56112/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56112/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56112/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56112","id":2581654236,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6Z4Orc","number":56112,"title":"Overhead in recursive broadcasting due to recursion limiting","user":{"login":"yaccos","id":22662263,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNjYyMjYz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22662263?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos","html_url":"https://github.com/yaccos","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yaccos/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-11T15:31:14Z","updated_at":"2024-10-11T15:31:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I am trying to implement broadcasting on a custom data type, and I experience overhead which I strongly suspect is due to recursion limiting. Here is a MWE using Julia 1.10.5:

```Julia
using BenchmarkTools

struct MyType{T} <: AbstractVector{Number}
    val::T
end

# Only intended for displaying the type
Base.getindex(x::MyType, idx) = x.val[idx]

Base.BroadcastStyle(::Type{<:MyType}) = Broadcast.Style{MyType}()


@inline Base.length(x::MyType) = length(x.val)
Base.size(x::MyType) = (length(x),)

@inline unpack_args(x::MyType) = x.val
@inline unpack_args(x::Broadcast.Broadcasted{Broadcast.Style{MyType}}) = Broadcast.broadcasted(x.f,unpack_args(x.args)...)
@inline unpack_args(x::Tuple) = map(element -> unpack_args(element), x)


@inline function Base.copy(bc::Broadcast.Broadcasted{Broadcast.Style{MyType}, Axes, F, Args}) where {Axes,F,Args<:Tuple}
    f = bc.f
    args = bc.args
    new_val = broadcast(f,unpack_args(args)...)
    MyType(new_val)
end

@inline function Base.copyto!(dest::MyType,bc::Broadcast.Broadcasted{Broadcast.Style{MyType}, Axes, F, Args}) where {Axes,F,Args<:Tuple}
    f = bc.f
    args = bc.args
    broadcast!(f, dest.val, unpack_args(args)...)
    dest
end

x = MyType([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])

function f(val, N)
    for i in 1:N
        val .+= val
    end
end

N = 1e9
```

```
julia> @btime f(x, N)
8.574 s (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
julia> @btime f(x.val, N)
2.048 s (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

In theory, it should be possible to optimize out the outer `broadcast!()` call on `MyType` and achieve the same performance as when broadcasting on the inner vector directly. Using `Cthulhu` and a profiler, it appears that broadcasting over `MyType` is type-stable, but the inner call to `broadcast!()` is slow because the next function call to `materialize()!` is not cached (reported as `< uncached >` and `Info: Inference didn't cache this call information because of imprecise analysis due to recursion` by `Cthulhu`). Even though this particular example could be made more efficient using using the default `copy()` and `copyto!()` methods of `AbstractArray`, that does not generalize to my intended use case which involves heterogeneous structures. Could this inefficiency be resolved by https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48059?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56112/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56112/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

am trying to implement broadcasting on a custom data type, and I experience overhead which I strongly suspect is due to recursion limiting. Here is a MWE using Julia 1.10.5:

```Julia
using BenchmarkTools

struct MyType{T} <: AbstractVector{Number}
    val::T
end

# Only intended for displaying the type
Base.getindex(x::MyType, idx) = x.val[idx]

Base.BroadcastStyle(::Type{<:MyType}) = Broadcast.Style{MyType}()


@inline Base.length(x::MyType) = length(x.val)
Base.size(x::MyType) = (length(x),)

@inline unpack_args(x::MyType) = x.val
@inline unpack_args(x::Broadcast.Broadcasted{Broadcast.Style{MyType}}) = Broadcast.broadcasted(x.f,unpack_args(x.args)...)
@inline unpack_args(x::Tuple) = map(element -> unpack_args(element), x)


@inline function Base.copy(bc::Broadcast.Broadcasted{Broadcast.Style{MyType}, Axes, F, Args}) where {Axes,F,Args<:Tuple}
    f = bc.f
    args = bc.args
    new_val = broadcast(f,unpack_args(args)...)
    MyType(new_val)
end

@inline function Base.copyto!(dest::MyType,bc::Broadcast.Broadcasted{Broadcast.Style{MyType}, Axes, F, Args}) where {Axes,F,Args<:Tuple}
    f = bc.f
    args = bc.args
    broadcast!(f, dest.val, unpack_args(args)...)
    dest
end

x = MyType([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])

function f(val, N)
    for i in 1:N
        val .+= val
    end
end

N = 1e9
```

```
julia> @btime f(x, N)
8.574 s (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
julia> @btime f(x.val, N)
2.048 s (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

In theory, it should be possible to optimize out the outer `broadcast!()` call on `MyType` and achieve the same performance as when broadcasting on the inner vector directly. Using `Cthulhu` and a profiler, it appears that broadcasting over `MyType` is type-stable, but the inner call to `broadcast!()` is slow because the next function call to `materialize()!` is not cached (reported as `< uncached >` and `Info: Inference didn't cache this call information because of imprecise analysis due to recursion` by `Cthulhu`). Even though this particular example could be made more efficient using using the default `copy()` and `copyto!()` methods of `AbstractArray`, that does not generalize to my intended use case which involves heterogeneous structures. When I refined

```Julia
@inline function Base.copyto!(dest::MyType,bc::Broadcast.Broadcasted{Broadcast.Style{MyType}, Axes, F, Args}) where {Axes,F,Args<:Tuple}
    f = bc.f
    args = bc.args
    dest.val .= f.(unpack_args(args)...)
    dest
end
```
the overhead went away within the margin of error for some stange reason:
```
julia> @btime f(x, N)
1.993 s (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
julia> @btime f(x.val, N)
1.976 s (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2410405836/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
